expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of the cardiac cycle—the sequence of electrical and mechanical events that occur during one heartbeat—detailing the electrical conduction system (SA node, internodal pathways, AV node, His bundle, bundle branches, Purkinje fibers), the phases of the cardiac cycle (atrial systole, ventricular systole, ventricular diastole), pressure-volume relationships, the electrocardiogram (ECG) as a recording of cardiac electrical activity, and the regulation of heart rate and contractility by the autonomic nervous system. Emphasis is placed on the coupling of electrical excitation to mechanical contraction (excitation-contraction coupling), the mechanisms of automaticity, and clinical correlations (arrhythmias, heart block, ECG interpretation, pacemakers).

core_concept.principle: The cardiac cycle is initiated by spontaneous depolarization of the sinoatrial (SA) node (pacemaker), which generates action potentials that propagate through the conduction system to coordinate atrial and ventricular contraction—ensuring efficient pumping: atrial systole completes ventricular filling, ventricular systole ejects blood, and ventricular diastole allows refilling—with the electrocardiogram (ECG) reflecting the electrical events (P wave, QRS complex, T wave) that precede mechanical events.
core_concept.explanation:
The cardiac cycle is the sequence of electrical and mechanical events that occur from the beginning of one heartbeat to the next. It is driven by the heart's intrinsic electrical conduction system and modulated by autonomic inputs. The cycle consists of systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation), subdivided into phases that ensure efficient, unidirectional blood flow.

Electrical Conduction System:
Sinoatrial (SA) Node: Natural pacemaker located in right atrium near SVC junction. Spontaneously depolarizes (phase 4 depolarization) at 60-100 bpm. Generates action potentials that spread through atria.
Internodal Pathways: Conduct impulses from SA node to AV node (anterior, middle, posterior tracts).
Atrioventricular (AV) Node: Located in interatrial septum near coronary sinus. Delays conduction (AV delay, ~0.1 sec) allowing atrial contraction to complete before ventricular activation.
His-Purkinje System: Bundle of His (penetrates cardiac skeleton), right and left bundle branches (run along interventricular septum), Purkinje fibers (subendocardial network). Rapidly conduct impulses to ventricular myocardium, enabling coordinated contraction.

Cardiac Cycle Phases:
Atrial Systole (late diastole): Atria contract → atrial pressure rises → AV valves open → additional blood ejected into ventricles (atrial kick, 20-30% of filling).
Isovolumetric Ventricular Contraction: Ventricles begin to contract → ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure → AV valves close (S1). Ventricular pressure < arterial pressure → semilunar valves remain closed. No volume change.
Rapid Ventricular Ejection: Ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure → semilunar valves open → blood ejected rapidly into aorta/pulmonary trunk.
Reduced Ventricular Ejection: Ventricular pressure begins to fall; ejection slows.
Isovolumetric Ventricular Relaxation: Ventricles relax → ventricular pressure < arterial pressure → semilunar valves close (S2). Ventricular pressure > atrial pressure → AV valves remain closed. No volume change.
Rapid Ventricular Filling: Ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure → AV valves open → blood flows rapidly from atria to ventricles (passive filling).
Reduced Ventricular Filling (diastasis): Slow filling until next atrial systole.

Electrocardiogram (ECG):
P Wave: Atrial depolarization (precedes atrial contraction).
QRS Complex: Ventricular depolarization (precedes ventricular contraction). Atrial repolarization is obscured.
T Wave: Ventricular repolarization.
PR Interval: Time from atrial depolarization to ventricular depolarization (includes AV delay).
QT Interval: Total duration of ventricular depolarization and repolarization.

Excitation-Contraction Coupling: Action potential depolarizes sarcolemma → voltage-gated L-type Ca²⁺ channels open → Ca²⁺ influx triggers Ca²⁺ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum (ryanodine receptors) → Ca²⁺ binds troponin C → cross-bridge cycling → contraction. Relaxation occurs when Ca²⁺ is pumped back into SR (SERCA) and extruded via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger.

Regulation of Heart Rate: Sympathetic (β1-adrenergic) → increases SA node firing rate (positive chronotropy), increases conduction velocity (positive dromotropy), increases contractility (positive inotropy). Parasympathetic (vagus, M2 muscarinic) → decreases SA node firing rate, decreases AV node conduction velocity.

Understanding the cardiac cycle is essential for clinical practice: ECG interpretation (arrhythmias, ischemia, conduction blocks), hemodynamic monitoring (pressures, volumes), and management of heart failure (optimizing preload, afterload, contractility).

image.alt: Timeline showing ECG (P wave, QRS, T wave), pressure tracings (aortic, ventricular, atrial), ventricular volume curve, and heart sounds (S1, S2). Phases: atrial systole, isovolumetric contraction, ejection, isovolumetric relaxation, rapid filling.
image.caption: Cardiac Cycle: Electrical events (ECG) precede mechanical events (pressure, volume, heart sounds). Phases include atrial systole (atrial kick), isovolumetric contraction, ejection, isovolumetric relaxation, and filling.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Heart's Electrical and Mechanical Coordination
sections.introduction:
The heartbeat is the result of precisely coordinated electrical and mechanical events. The heart generates its own rhythmic electrical impulses (automaticity) through specialized pacemaker cells; these impulses spread through the conduction system, triggering sequential contraction of atria and ventricles. The electrocardiogram (ECG) records these electrical events, providing a window into cardiac function. The mechanical events—pressure changes, volume changes, and valve movements—follow the electrical events with a short delay (excitation-contraction coupling).

The cardiac cycle is the complete sequence of events from one heartbeat to the next. It is typically divided into systole (contraction, ejection) and diastole (relaxation, filling). At rest, the cycle length is approximately 800-1000 ms (heart rate 60-75 bpm). Atrial systole occupies the last 100 ms of diastole; ventricular systole occupies approximately 300 ms; ventricular diastole occupies the remainder.

The electrical conduction system has intrinsic automaticity: SA node (60-100 bpm), AV node (40-60 bpm), Purkinje fibers (20-40 bpm). The SA node is the dominant pacemaker because it depolarizes fastest; its rate is modulated by the autonomic nervous system. Sympathetic stimulation increases heart rate (positive chronotropy); parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation decreases heart rate.

Understanding the cardiac cycle is essential for clinical practice: ECG interpretation (arrhythmias, ischemia, conduction defects), hemodynamic monitoring (pressures, volumes), and treatment of heart failure (modulating preload, afterload, contractility).

introduction_image.alt: Diagram of the heart showing conduction system: SA node (right atrium), internodal pathways, AV node (interatrial septum), bundle of His, right and left bundle branches, Purkinje fibers. Inset shows action potentials from different conduction system regions.
introduction_image.caption: Cardiac Conduction System: SA node (pacemaker) initiates impulses; AV node delays conduction; His-Purkinje system rapidly conducts to ventricles, ensuring coordinated contraction.

key_concept.definition: The cardiac cycle is initiated by spontaneous depolarization of the SA node, followed by sequential activation of atria (P wave), AV delay, ventricular depolarization (QRS), and ventricular repolarization (T wave)—with mechanical events (contraction, ejection, filling) occurring after electrical activation.
key_concept.context:
The electrical events recorded on ECG precede mechanical events: atrial depolarization (P wave) precedes atrial contraction; ventricular depolarization (QRS) precedes ventricular contraction; ventricular repolarization (T wave) precedes ventricular relaxation. The PR interval reflects AV nodal delay, allowing time for atrial contraction to complete before ventricular contraction. The QT interval reflects the duration of ventricular depolarization and repolarization; prolongation increases risk of arrhythmias (torsades de pointes).

Clinical relevance: ECG abnormalities localize conduction system pathology: first-degree AV block (PR >200 ms) indicates delayed AV conduction; second-degree AV block (Mobitz I, II) indicates intermittent conduction failure; third-degree (complete) heart block indicates complete dissociation between atria and ventricles. Bundle branch block (wide QRS) indicates delayed conduction through right or left bundle. Arrhythmias (atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia) reflect abnormal impulse generation or conduction.

image.alt: ECG tracing (P wave, PR interval, QRS complex, ST segment, T wave, QT interval) aligned with pressure tracings and heart sounds. Correlation between electrical and mechanical events.
image.caption: ECG and Cardiac Cycle: P wave (atrial depolarization) precedes atrial contraction; QRS (ventricular depolarization) precedes ventricular contraction; T wave (ventricular repolarization) precedes ventricular relaxation.

sections.key_concept: The cardiac cycle consists of distinct phases determined by pressure gradients across valves; the ECG reflects the electrical activity that triggers these mechanical events; heart rate and contractility are regulated by autonomic inputs.

classifications.type: Electrical Conduction System: Automaticity and Propagation
classifications.value: Best for understanding the generation and spread of cardiac electrical impulses, the basis of the ECG, and arrhythmias.
characteristics: Sinoatrial (SA) Node: Located at junction of SVC and right atrium. Primary pacemaker. Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization (pacemaker potential) due to funny current (If, Na⁺ influx) and decaying K⁺ conductance. Firing rate: 60-100 bpm (intrinsic). Sympathetic (β1) increases slope of phase 4 → increased rate; parasympathetic (M2) decreases slope → decreased rate.
Internodal Pathways: Anterior, middle, posterior tracts conduct impulses from SA node to AV node. Conduction velocity ~1 m/s.

Atrioventricular (AV) Node: Located in interatrial septum, near coronary sinus. Slows conduction (delay 0.1 sec) due to smaller cells with fewer gap junctions. Intrinsic rate: 40-60 bpm (escape pacemaker). Critical for allowing atrial contraction to complete before ventricular activation.

His-Purkinje System: Bundle of His (penetrates fibrous trigone), right and left bundle branches (along interventricular septum), Purkinje fibers (subendocardial network). Large cells with rapid conduction velocity (2-4 m/s). Intrinsic rate: 20-40 bpm.

Action Potentials: Pacemaker cells (SA, AV node): Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization (funny current If), phase 0 (Ca²⁺ influx via L-type channels), no fast Na⁺ channels. Ventricular myocytes: Phase 0 (rapid Na⁺ influx), phase 1 (transient K⁺ efflux), phase 2 (plateau, L-type Ca²⁺ influx balances K⁺ efflux), phase 3 (K⁺ efflux, repolarization), phase 4 (resting potential, stable).
classifications.behavior:
The SA node depolarizes spontaneously, generating an action potential that spreads through the atria (P wave on ECG). The impulse reaches the AV node, where conduction slows (PR interval). After the AV node, the impulse travels rapidly through the His-Purkinje system, activating the ventricular myocardium from endocardium to epicardium (QRS complex). Ventricular repolarization occurs (T wave).

Autonomic modulation: Sympathetic stimulation (norepinephrine, β1 receptors) increases SA node firing rate (positive chronotropy), increases AV node conduction velocity (positive dromotropy), and increases ventricular contractility (positive inotropy) by enhancing Ca²⁺ influx. Parasympathetic stimulation (acetylcholine, M2 receptors) decreases SA node firing rate, decreases AV node conduction velocity, and slightly decreases atrial contractility.

Clinical correlations: Sinus tachycardia (HR >100 bpm) can be physiologic (exercise) or pathologic (fever, heart failure, hyperthyroidism). Sinus bradycardia (HR <60 bpm) can be physiologic (athletes) or pathologic (hypothyroidism, increased vagal tone, medications). Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves) results from chaotic atrial activation; AV node conducts irregularly. Heart block: first-degree (PR >200 ms); second-degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach, progressive PR prolongation until dropped beat) usually AV node disease; second-degree Mobitz II (fixed PR with intermittent dropped beats) usually His-Purkinje disease; third-degree (complete heart block, atria and ventricles dissociated).

examples.process: Atrioventricular (AV) Node Delay and Heart Block
examples.explanation:
AV node delay is critical for coordinating atrial and ventricular contraction: (1) Normal AV delay: 100-200 ms (PR interval on ECG). Allows atrial contraction (atrial kick) to complete before ventricular contraction. (2) First-degree AV block: PR interval >200 ms; all impulses conduct; usually benign, may indicate increased vagal tone or AV node disease. (3) Second-degree AV block, Mobitz I (Wenckebach): Progressive PR prolongation until a dropped QRS; usually AV node level; often benign. (4) Second-degree AV block, Mobitz II: Fixed PR interval with intermittent dropped QRS; usually infra-Hisian (His-Purkinje system); high risk of progression to complete heart block; often requires pacemaker. (5) Third-degree (complete) AV block: No atrial impulses reach ventricles; atria and ventricles beat independently; escape rhythm (junctional or ventricular) maintains cardiac output; symptoms include syncope, heart failure; requires pacemaker.

Significance: AV conduction abnormalities illustrate the importance of the AV node as a gatekeeper, and the clinical consequences of delayed or blocked conduction.

image.alt: ECG examples: first-degree AV block (PR >200 ms); second-degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach, progressive PR prolongation); second-degree Mobitz II (fixed PR, dropped QRS); third-degree complete heart block (P waves and QRS dissociated).
image.caption: AV Block: First-degree (delayed conduction), second-degree (intermittent conduction), third-degree (complete block). ECG patterns reflect the level and severity of conduction delay.

classifications.type: Mechanical Phases: Pressure, Volume, and Heart Sounds
classifications.value: Best for understanding the hemodynamic events of the cardiac cycle, the relationship between pressure and volume, and the origin of heart sounds.
characteristics: Atrial Systole (Late Diastole): Atria contract → atrial pressure (a wave) rises → AV valves open → additional blood ejected into ventricles (atrial kick). Contributes 20-30% of ventricular filling. ECG: P wave. Heart sound: none (S4 if pathologic, e.g., reduced compliance).

Isovolumetric Ventricular Contraction: Ventricles contract → ventricular pressure rises rapidly. When ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure → AV valves close (S1). Ventricular pressure still below arterial pressure → semilunar valves remain closed. No volume change (isovolumetric). ECG: QRS onset to semilunar valve opening.

Rapid Ventricular Ejection: Ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure → semilunar valves open → rapid ejection of blood (70% of stroke volume). Ventricular pressure peaks (LV ~120 mmHg, RV ~25 mmHg).
Reduced Ventricular Ejection: Ventricular pressure begins to fall; ejection slows (remaining 30% of stroke volume).

Isovolumetric Ventricular Relaxation: Ventricles relax → ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure → semilunar valves close (S2). Ventricular pressure still above atrial pressure → AV valves remain closed. No volume change. ECG: T wave (repolarization).
Rapid Ventricular Filling: Ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure → AV valves open → blood flows rapidly from atria to ventricles (passive filling, 70-80% of ventricular volume).
Reduced Ventricular Filling (Diastasis): Slow filling until next atrial systole.

Heart Sounds: S1 (lub)—AV valve closure (beginning of systole). S2 (dub)—semilunar valve closure (beginning of diastole). S3—rapid filling (normal in children, pathologic in adults → volume overload). S4—atrial contraction (pathologic → reduced compliance).
classifications.behavior:
The cardiac cycle phases are defined by pressure gradients across valves. At the onset of systole, ventricular pressure rises rapidly, closing AV valves (S1). When ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure, semilunar valves open, and ejection occurs. At the end of systole, ventricular relaxation causes pressure to fall below arterial pressure, closing semilunar valves (S2). When ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure, AV valves open, and filling begins.

Pressure-volume relationships: The pressure-volume loop plots LV pressure against volume throughout the cycle. End-diastolic volume (EDV) is the volume before contraction; end-systolic volume (ESV) is the volume after ejection; stroke volume (SV) = EDV - ESV; ejection fraction (EF) = SV/EDV. Preload (end-diastolic volume) determines stroke volume via Frank-Starling mechanism; afterload (arterial pressure) opposes ejection; contractility (inotropy) increases stroke volume at given preload and afterload.

Clinical correlations: Heart failure with reduced EF (HFrEF) shows reduced contractility, increased EDV, increased ESV, reduced EF. Diastolic heart failure (HFpEF) shows preserved EF but impaired relaxation (reduced compliance), elevated filling pressures, S4 gallop. Aortic stenosis causes pressure overload → increased LV pressure, concentric hypertrophy, S4. Mitral regurgitation causes volume overload → increased LV volume, eccentric hypertrophy, S3 gallop.

examples.explanation:
The pressure-volume (PV) loop is a comprehensive representation of ventricular function: (1) Loop points: A (end-diastole, mitral valve closes) → B (isovolumetric contraction) → C (aortic valve opens) → D (end-systole, aortic valve closes) → A (isovolumetric relaxation). (2) Stroke volume = EDV - ESV (width of loop). (3) Preload = EDV; afterload = systolic pressure; contractility = slope of end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR). (4) Changes: Increased preload (volume infusion) → increased EDV, increased SV (Frank-Starling). Increased afterload (aortic stenosis) → increased systolic pressure, decreased SV unless compensatory hypertrophy. Increased contractility (dobutamine) → increased EF, decreased ESV, increased SV. (5) Clinical application: PV loops are measured invasively (conductance catheters) or estimated by echocardiography to assess ventricular function and guide therapy in heart failure, valvular disease, and mechanical circulatory support.

Significance: The PV loop integrates preload, afterload, and contractility into a single graphical representation, enabling quantitative assessment of ventricular function.

image.alt: Pressure-volume loop: points (A-D), end-diastolic volume (EDV), end-systolic volume (ESV), stroke volume (SV). Effects of increased preload, afterload, and contractility on loop shape and position.
image.caption: Pressure-Volume Loop: Graphical representation of ventricular function. Stroke volume (EDV-ESV), preload (EDV), afterload (systolic pressure), and contractility (ESPVR slope) are assessed.

classifications.type: Excitation-Contraction Coupling: From Electrical to Mechanical
classifications.value: Best for understanding how the electrical signal (action potential) triggers mechanical contraction, and the regulation of contractility.
characteristics: Action Potential (Ventricular Myocyte): Phase 0 (rapid Na⁺ influx), phase 1 (transient K⁺ efflux), phase 2 (plateau, L-type Ca²⁺ influx balances K⁺ efflux), phase 3 (K⁺ efflux, repolarization), phase 4 (resting potential).

Calcium-Induced Calcium Release: Action potential depolarizes sarcolemma → L-type Ca²⁺ channels (dihydropyridine receptors) open → Ca²⁺ influx (trigger). Ca²⁺ binds ryanodine receptors (RyR2) on sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) → SR releases large amount of Ca²⁺ (calcium-induced calcium release).
Contraction: Cytosolic Ca²⁺ rises from ~100 nM to ~1 μM → Ca²⁺ binds troponin C → conformational change moves tropomyosin, exposing myosin-binding sites on actin → cross-bridge cycling → contraction.

Relaxation: Ca²⁺ is pumped back into SR by SERCA2a (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase) and extruded from cell by Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX). Phospholamban regulates SERCA2a; phosphorylation (by PKA) disinhibits SERCA, increasing relaxation rate (lusitropy).

Regulation of Contractility: Sympathetic stimulation (β1, PKA) phosphorylates L-type Ca²⁺ channels (increases Ca²⁺ influx), phospholamban (increases SR Ca²⁺ uptake), troponin I (decreases Ca²⁺ sensitivity, enhances relaxation), and ryanodine receptors (increases SR Ca²⁺ release). Net effect: increased contractility (positive inotropy) and increased relaxation rate (positive lusitropy).
classifications.behavior:
Excitation-contraction coupling links the electrical signal to mechanical contraction. The plateau phase (phase 2) of the ventricular action potential is critical for maintaining Ca²⁺ influx and sustaining contraction. The long refractory period (absolute refractory period ~250 ms) prevents tetanus (summation of contractions) and allows time for ventricular filling.

Inotropic agents: Sympathetic stimulation (norepinephrine, dobutamine) increases contractility via β1 receptors → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylation of key proteins. Digoxin inhibits Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase → increased intracellular Na⁺ → reduced NCX activity → increased intracellular Ca²⁺ → increased contractility. Calcium sensitizers (levosimendan) increase contractility without increasing Ca²⁺ by sensitizing troponin C.

Clinical correlations: Heart failure with reduced contractility (systolic dysfunction) is treated with inotropic agents (dobutamine) for acute decompensation, and with β-blockers (chronic) to reduce sympathetic overstimulation. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) involves abnormal Ca²⁺ handling, leading to hypercontractility and impaired relaxation. Sudden cardiac death in HCM may result from arrhythmias. Calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are used in HCM to improve relaxation and reduce contractility.

examples.explanation:
Abnormal calcium cycling is central to heart failure pathophysiology: (1) In heart failure, there is decreased SERCA2a expression and increased phospholamban inhibition → reduced SR Ca²⁺ uptake → prolonged Ca²⁺ transient → impaired relaxation (diastolic dysfunction) and reduced SR Ca²⁺ load → reduced contractility (systolic dysfunction). (2) Additionally, increased RyR2 leak (due to PKA hyperphosphorylation) depletes SR Ca²⁺ stores and may trigger arrhythmias. (3) Therapeutic approaches: SERCA2a gene therapy (investigational); phospholamban inhibition; RyR2 stabilization (investigational). (4) Current therapies: β-blockers reduce sympathetic stimulation, normalizing PKA hyperphosphorylation; digoxin increases Ca²⁺ indirectly; inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) increase cAMP but may increase mortality in chronic use.

Significance: Understanding calcium cycling in heart failure has led to novel therapeutic targets and explains the pathophysiology of systolic and diastolic dysfunction.

image.alt: Calcium cycling in normal myocyte: L-type Ca²⁺ channel, RyR2, SERCA2a. In heart failure: decreased SERCA2a, increased phospholamban, RyR2 leak → reduced SR Ca²⁺ load, impaired relaxation, reduced contractility.
image.caption: Calcium Cycling in Heart Failure: Reduced SERCA2a and increased RyR2 leak impair relaxation and reduce contractility. β-blockers normalize sympathetic overdrive; SERCA2a gene therapy is an investigational approach.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of the Cardiac Cycle

principles.principle: The Heart is an Electrical Syncytium: Gap Junctions Enable Coordinated Contraction
principles.statement: Cardiac muscle cells are electrically coupled by gap junctions (connexin 43, 40, 45), forming a functional syncytium—atria and ventricles each behave as a single electrical unit, allowing coordinated, synchronized contraction that maximizes pump efficiency.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Gap junctions allow action potentials to spread rapidly from cell to cell without requiring innervation of each myocyte. (2) The atria are electrically isolated from the ventricles by the fibrous cardiac skeleton; only the AV node provides electrical continuity. (3) The His-Purkinje system ensures rapid, simultaneous activation of the ventricular mass, producing a coordinated contraction wave from apex to base. (4) Gap junction distribution is heterogeneous: more connexin 43 in ventricles; connexin 40 in atria and Purkinje fibers.

Clinical application: Gap junction remodeling occurs in heart disease: reduced connexin 43 expression in heart failure and myocardial infarction increases arrhythmia risk. Conduction slowing in diseased myocardium can lead to re-entry circuits (e.g., ventricular tachycardia after MI). Antiarrhythmic drugs affect gap junction function and conduction velocity.
principles.example:
Ventricular tachycardia after myocardial infarction: (1) MI causes necrosis of ventricular myocardium, replaced by fibrotic scar. (2) Scar tissue disrupts normal conduction pathways, creating areas of slow conduction. (3) Re-entry circuits can form where electrical impulses travel around scar tissue, re-exciting previously activated areas. (4) This re-entrant circuit can sustain rapid ventricular tachycardia (VT). (5) Treatment: Antiarrhythmic drugs (amiodarone, lidocaine) slow conduction or prolong refractoriness; catheter ablation destroys the re-entry circuit; implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) terminates VT.

Significance: The electrical syncytium enables coordinated contraction but also provides substrate for re-entrant arrhythmias when conduction is disrupted by scar or ischemia.
principles.analogy: The heart is like a stadium wave: Gap junctions are like the fans passing the wave to their neighbors. The wave travels in a coordinated pattern (SA node to atria to AV node to ventricles). If some fans are missing or don't pass the wave (scar), the wave may go around them, causing a re-entrant wave (like fans starting a second wave that goes around the stadium).

image.alt: Cardiac myocytes connected by gap junctions (connexin 43). Electrical propagation from cell to cell. Scar tissue creating re-entry circuit (VT).
image.caption: Cardiac Electrical Syncytium: Gap junctions enable rapid cell-to-cell propagation. Scar tissue disrupts normal conduction, creating re-entry circuits that cause ventricular tachycardia.

principles.principle: Frank-Starling Mechanism: Preload Determines Stroke Volume
principles.statement: The Frank-Starling mechanism states that the stroke volume of the heart increases in response to an increase in end-diastolic volume (preload)—the greater the stretch of ventricular muscle fibers during filling, the greater the force of contraction, enabling the heart to match output to venous return.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Increased venous return → increased EDV → increased sarcomere stretch → increased actin-myosin overlap → increased Ca²⁺ sensitivity → increased force generation → increased stroke volume. (2) This mechanism balances output of the right and left ventricles: changes in venous return to right heart are matched by left heart output after a few beats. (3) The Frank-Starling curve (cardiac function curve) plots stroke volume vs. EDV (or ventricular filling pressure).

Clinical application: In heart failure, the Frank-Starling curve is flattened and shifted downward: a given increase in preload produces less increase in stroke volume. Diuretics (reduce preload) are used to relieve pulmonary congestion (decrease EDV) but may reduce stroke volume if preload is too low. In hypovolemic shock, reduced preload decreases stroke volume; fluid resuscitation increases EDV and stroke volume via Frank-Starling.
principles.example:
Patient with heart failure and pulmonary edema: (1) Reduced contractility → decreased stroke volume at given preload → increased EDV (ventricular dilation) → increased filling pressures → pulmonary congestion (dyspnea, orthopnea). (2) Diuretics (furosemide) reduce venous return (preload) → decrease EDV → reduce pulmonary congestion. (3) However, excessive preload reduction can decrease stroke volume (Starling curve). (4) Inotropic agents (dobutamine) increase contractility, shifting the Starling curve upward, increasing stroke volume at any given preload. (5) Goal: Balance preload reduction (decongestion) with maintenance of adequate stroke volume.

Significance: The Frank-Starling mechanism explains how the heart adapts to changes in venous return and is central to understanding heart failure management (preload reduction vs. inotropic support).
principles.analogy: The Frank-Starling mechanism is like a rubber band: A rubber band stretched to a certain length (preload) will snap back with more force than a loose band. But if overstretched, it may not contract as effectively (heart failure). The ideal stretch maximizes contraction force.

image.alt: Frank-Starling curve (cardiac function curve): stroke volume vs. end-diastolic volume. Normal curve (upward slope). Heart failure curve (flattened, downward shift). Effects of diuretics (reduce preload) and inotropes (increase contractility).
image.caption: Frank-Starling Mechanism: Stroke volume increases with end-diastolic volume (preload). In heart failure, the curve is flattened; diuretics reduce preload, inotropes increase contractility.

principles.principle: Autonomic Regulation: Sympathetic and Parasympathetic Balance
principles.statement: Heart rate and contractility are regulated by the autonomic nervous system: sympathetic (β1-adrenergic) increases rate (chronotropy), conduction velocity (dromotropy), and contractility (inotropy); parasympathetic (vagal, M2 muscarinic) decreases rate and conduction velocity, with minimal effect on ventricular contractility.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Sympathetic tone increases during exercise, stress, and hypotension; parasympathetic tone dominates at rest. (2) Sympathetic stimulation increases SA node firing rate (positive chronotropy) by increasing slope of phase 4 depolarization (increased If and Ca²⁺ current). (3) Sympathetic increases AV node conduction velocity (shortens PR interval) and increases ventricular contractility (positive inotropy) via PKA phosphorylation. (4) Parasympathetic stimulation decreases SA node firing rate (negative chronotropy) by increasing K⁺ conductance (hyperpolarization) and decreasing If; decreases AV node conduction velocity (negative dromotropy).

Clinical application: β-blockers (metoprolol, carvedilol) are used in heart failure, hypertension, and arrhythmias to reduce sympathetic overactivity. Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid massage) increase parasympathetic tone, terminating supraventricular tachycardias (AV nodal re-entry). Atropine (anticholinergic) blocks vagal tone, increasing heart rate in bradycardia. Exercise stress testing assesses chronotropic competence.
principles.example:
β-blockers in heart failure with reduced EF (HFrEF): (1) Chronic heart failure is characterized by sympathetic overactivation, which initially compensates for reduced contractility (increased heart rate, increased contractility). (2) However, chronic sympathetic activation is maladaptive: causes myocyte hypertrophy, apoptosis, and arrhythmias. (3) β-blockers (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol succinate) block β1-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, reducing contractility, and preventing adverse remodeling. (4) Paradoxically, β-blockers improve symptoms and reduce mortality in HFrEF despite their negative inotropic effect; the benefits derive from reducing sympathetic toxicity and allowing favorable remodeling. (5) β-blockers are initiated at low doses and slowly titrated to target doses.

Significance: β-blockers in heart failure illustrate the complex role of autonomic regulation—acute sympathetic stimulation is beneficial (supports failing heart), but chronic overactivation is harmful. Pharmacologic modulation of autonomic tone is a cornerstone of cardiovascular therapy.
principles.analogy: The autonomic regulation of the heart is like a car's gas pedal and brake: Sympathetic is the gas pedal (speeds up, increases power); parasympathetic is the brake (slows down). In heart failure, the gas pedal is stuck (chronic sympathetic activation), which damages the engine. β-blockers partially release the gas pedal, allowing the engine to recover.

image.alt: Autonomic innervation of heart: sympathetic (β1 receptors) increases rate, contractility; parasympathetic (vagus, M2 receptors) decreases rate, AV conduction. β-blockers block sympathetic effects.
image.caption: Autonomic Regulation: Sympathetic (β1) increases heart rate and contractility; parasympathetic (vagal) decreases heart rate. β-blockers reduce sympathetic overactivity in heart failure.

sections.section_title: Key Components of the Cardiac Cycle

components.definition: Electrical: SA node, internodal pathways, AV node, His bundle, bundle branches, Purkinje fibers. Mechanical: atria, ventricles, AV valves, semilunar valves. Hemodynamic: pressures (atrial, ventricular, arterial), volumes (EDV, ESV, SV), EF. ECG: P wave, PR interval, QRS complex, ST segment, T wave, QT interval.
components.equation: Heart Rate (HR) = 60 / RR interval (seconds); Cardiac Output (CO) = HR × Stroke Volume (SV); Ejection Fraction (EF) = SV / EDV × 100%
components.interpretation: Cardiac output is the product of heart rate and stroke volume. At rest, CO is 4-6 L/min; during exercise, it can increase to 20-25 L/min. Ejection fraction is the fraction of end-diastolic volume ejected with each beat; normal 55-70%. EF <40% indicates systolic heart failure. Heart rate is determined by SA node automaticity and autonomic tone.

image.alt: Summary diagram: conduction system (SA node, AV node, Purkinje); ECG waveform; pressure tracings; volume curve; heart sounds. Key parameters: HR, SV, CO, EF.
image.caption: Cardiac Cycle Components: Electrical (conduction system, ECG), mechanical (pressure, volume, heart sounds), and functional (HR, SV, CO, EF) parameters are integrated.

categories.description: The ECG records the electrical activity of the heart from the body surface, providing information about heart rate, rhythm, conduction intervals, and myocardial ischemia/infarction.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
ECG leads: Standard limb leads (I, II, III), augmented limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF), precordial leads (V1-V6). Each lead records the electrical vector from a specific orientation.

Waves and Intervals:
P Wave: Atrial depolarization (normal duration <120 ms, amplitude <2.5 mm).
PR Interval: Atrial depolarization to ventricular depolarization (normal 120-200 ms); reflects AV node conduction.
QRS Complex: Ventricular depolarization (normal duration <100 ms); wide QRS (>120 ms) indicates bundle branch block or ventricular origin.
ST Segment: Early ventricular repolarization; elevation indicates myocardial injury; depression indicates ischemia.
T Wave: Ventricular repolarization.
QT Interval: Total duration of ventricular depolarization and repolarization (normal <440 ms in males, <460 ms in females); prolonged QT increases risk of torsades de pointes.
U Wave: Uncertain significance; prominent in hypokalemia.

examples.explanation:
ECG is critical for diagnosing acute myocardial infarction (STEMI): (1) ST-segment elevation (≥1 mm in limb leads, ≥2 mm in precordial leads) indicates transmural injury. (2) Localization: ST elevation in V1-V4 (anterior MI, LAD occlusion); II, III, aVF (inferior MI, RCA occlusion); I, aVL, V5-V6 (lateral MI, LCx occlusion). (3) Reciprocal ST depression in opposite leads (e.g., inferior MI may show ST depression in aVL). (4) Pathologic Q waves (≥0.04 sec, ≥1/3 QRS amplitude) indicate established infarction. (5) T wave inversion may indicate ischemia. (6) Clinical: STEMI requires emergent reperfusion (PCI or thrombolysis) to salvage myocardium.

Significance: ECG localization of MI guides acute management, identifying the occluded coronary artery and determining treatment strategy.

image.alt: ECG leads and coronary territories: V1-V4 (LAD, anterior MI); II, III, aVF (RCA, inferior MI); I, aVL, V5-V6 (LCx, lateral MI). ST elevation patterns.
image.caption: ECG in Myocardial Infarction: ST-segment elevation localizes the infarct territory and identifies the occluded coronary artery, guiding reperfusion therapy.

categories.description: Arrhythmias are disorders of impulse formation (automaticity) or impulse conduction, ranging from benign to life-threatening.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Bradyarrhythmias: Sinus bradycardia (HR <60), AV block (first, second, third degree), sick sinus syndrome. Tachyarrhythmias: Supraventricular (atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia—AVNRT, AV re-entrant tachycardia—AVRT), ventricular (ventricular tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation).

Mechanisms: Abnormal automaticity (increased phase 4 slope, e.g., ischemia); triggered activity (early or delayed afterdepolarizations, e.g., digoxin toxicity); re-entry (circus movement around an obstacle, e.g., scar-related VT, AVNRT).

examples.process: Atrial Fibrillation: Most Common Sustained Arrhythmia
examples.explanation:
Atrial fibrillation (AF) is characterized by chaotic atrial activation and irregular ventricular response: (1) Pathophysiology: Multiple re-entrant wavelets in atria; triggers often from pulmonary vein foci. (2) ECG: Irregularly irregular rhythm, no P waves (fibrillatory waves), QRS normal (if no bundle branch block). (3) Consequences: Loss of atrial kick → reduced cardiac output (especially in diastolic dysfunction); stasis in atria → increased risk of thromboembolism (stroke). (4) Rate control: β-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin to control ventricular rate. (5) Rhythm control: Antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, flecainide) or catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation). (6) Anticoagulation: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) or warfarin to prevent stroke; risk stratification using CHA₂DS₂-VASc score.

Significance: Atrial fibrillation illustrates the clinical importance of arrhythmias (symptoms, hemodynamic compromise, stroke risk) and the multiple therapeutic approaches (rate control, rhythm control, anticoagulation).

image.alt: ECG of atrial fibrillation: irregularly irregular, no P waves. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score for stroke risk. Treatment: rate control (β-blockers), rhythm control (ablation), anticoagulation.
image.caption: Atrial Fibrillation: Chaotic atrial activation causes irregular ventricular response, loss of atrial kick, and stroke risk. Management includes rate control, rhythm control, and anticoagulation.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Cardiac Cycle Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Cardiac Cycle Case Analysis: ECG and Hemodynamics
worked_examples.problem: A 55-year-old man presents with dizziness and near-syncope. ECG shows a heart rate of 38 bpm, regular P waves (P-P interval constant), PR interval of 280 ms, and QRS complexes that occur independently of P waves (no relationship). (1) What is the diagnosis? (2) What is the mechanism? (3) What are the hemodynamic consequences? (4) What is the treatment?

step_by_step.calculation: Heart rate 38 bpm (bradycardia). Regular P waves (sinus rhythm). PR interval 280 ms (prolonged) but variable? Actually, QRS complexes occur independently of P waves (no relationship) → atria and ventricles are dissociated. Ventricular rate 38 bpm (escape rhythm). This is third-degree (complete) AV block.

Complete AV block means no atrial impulses reach the ventricles. The AV node or His-Purkinje system fails to conduct. Ventricular activation is maintained by an escape rhythm—a subsidiary pacemaker (junctional at 40-60 bpm or Purkinje at 20-40 bpm). In this patient, ventricular rate 38 bpm suggests a junctional or high Purkinje escape rhythm. Causes include degenerative fibrosis (Lev's disease, Lenègre's disease), ischemic heart disease, medications (β-blockers, calcium channel blockers), or infiltrative diseases.

Atrial contraction no longer contributes to ventricular filling (loss of atrial kick) → reduced EDV and stroke volume, especially in patients with diastolic dysfunction. The slow ventricular rate (38 bpm) further reduces cardiac output. Symptoms: fatigue, dizziness, syncope (especially with exertion when cardiac output cannot increase). Risk of asystole if escape rhythm fails.

Third-degree AV block with symptoms requires pacemaker implantation. Temporary pacing (transcutaneous or transvenous) may be needed acutely. Permanent pacemaker: dual-chamber (DDD) restores AV synchrony (atrial sensing, ventricular pacing) and provides rate-responsive pacing. If the patient is asymptomatic and the escape rhythm is reliable (rare), observation may be considered, but most require pacing.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Diagnosis: Third-degree (complete) AV block. (2) Mechanism: No atrial impulses conducted to ventricles; ventricular escape rhythm (junctional or Purkinje) maintains cardiac output. (3) Hemodynamic consequences: Loss of atrial kick (reduced EDV), slow ventricular rate (reduced CO), symptoms of low output (dizziness, near-syncope). (4) Treatment: Permanent pacemaker implantation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding AV conduction, the ECG correlates of conduction block, and the hemodynamic consequences of AV dissociation guides diagnosis and management of complete heart block. Pacemaker therapy restores AV synchrony and prevents symptoms.

sections.introduction: The cardiac cycle is a continuous sequence of electrical and mechanical events. This flow integrates the ECG, pressure changes, volume changes, and valve movements.

process_flow.description: SA node fires; atria depolarize (P wave); atria contract (atrial systole).
process_flow.mechanism: SA node depolarization spreads through atria (P wave). Atrial contraction increases atrial pressure (a wave), forcing additional blood into ventricles (atrial kick, 20-30% filling). AV valves are open; semilunar valves closed.

image.alt: ECG: P wave. Atrial contraction; blood flow from atria to ventricles. Atrial pressure a wave.
image.caption: Step 1: Atrial Depolarization and Contraction. P wave (atrial depolarization) precedes atrial contraction, which delivers the atrial kick and completes ventricular filling (EDV).

process_flow.description: Ventricles depolarize (QRS); ventricles contract; AV valves close (S1); no ejection yet.
process_flow.mechanism: Ventricular depolarization (QRS) triggers contraction. Ventricular pressure rises rapidly; when ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure, AV valves close (S1). Ventricular pressure still below arterial pressure → semilunar valves remain closed. No volume change (isovolumetric).
process_flow.result: AV valves close; ventricular pressure rising; no ejection

image.alt: ECG: QRS complex. Ventricular contraction; AV valves close (S1); no ejection; isovolumetric phase.
image.caption: Step 2: Isovolumetric Ventricular Contraction. QRS (ventricular depolarization) triggers contraction. AV valves close (S1); semilunar valves remain closed; ventricular volume constant.

process_flow.description: Ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure; semilunar valves open; blood ejected into aorta/pulmonary trunk.
process_flow.mechanism: When ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure, semilunar valves open. Rapid ejection (70% of stroke volume) followed by reduced ejection. Ventricular pressure peaks (LV ~120 mmHg, RV ~25 mmHg). Aortic pressure rises (120/80 mmHg).
process_flow.result: Blood ejected into systemic and pulmonary circulations

image.alt: Semilunar valves open; blood ejected into aorta; aortic pressure rises; ventricular volume decreases (SV).
image.caption: Step 3: Ventricular Ejection. When ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure, semilunar valves open. Blood is ejected, reducing ventricular volume (stroke volume).

process_flow.description: Ventricles repolarize (T wave); ventricles relax; semilunar valves close (S2); AV valves remain closed.
process_flow.mechanism: Ventricular repolarization (T wave) precedes relaxation. When ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure, semilunar valves close (S2). Ventricular pressure still above atrial pressure → AV valves remain closed. No volume change (isovolumetric). End-systolic volume (ESV) is the volume remaining after ejection.

image.alt: ECG: T wave. Ventricular relaxation; semilunar valves close (S2); isovolumetric relaxation; ESV.
image.caption: Step 4: Isovolumetric Ventricular Relaxation. T wave (ventricular repolarization) precedes relaxation. Semilunar valves close (S2); ventricular volume constant (ESV).

process_flow.description: Ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure; AV valves open; blood flows into ventricles.
process_flow.mechanism: When ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure, AV valves open. Rapid passive filling (70-80% of ventricular volume) followed by slow filling (diastasis). Ventricular volume increases toward EDV.
process_flow.result: Ventricles fill; cycle ready to repeat

image.alt: AV valves open; blood flows from atria to ventricles; ventricular volume increases (filling).
image.caption: Step 5: Ventricular Filling. When ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure, AV valves open. Blood flows passively into ventricles (rapid filling), completing the cycle.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Cardiac Electrophysiology and Arrhythmia Mechanisms
sections.definition: Cardiac arrhythmias arise from disorders of impulse formation (abnormal automaticity, triggered activity) or impulse conduction (re-entry). Understanding these mechanisms enables targeted antiarrhythmic therapy and catheter ablation.

process_types.type: Re-entry: The Most Common Arrhythmia Mechanism
process_types.energy_profile: Re-entry occurs when an electrical impulse travels around a circuit (anatomic or functional) and re-excites tissue after its refractory period, creating a self-sustaining circus movement.
process_types.direction: Requirements for re-entry: (1) A circuit (anatomic or functional). (2) Unidirectional block in one pathway. (3) Slow conduction in the other pathway, allowing the blocked pathway to recover excitability. (4) Return of the impulse to re-excite the originally activated tissue. Examples: AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT), AV re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT, WPW), atrial flutter (cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent), ventricular tachycardia (scar-related).
process_types.function: Re-entry explains most sustained tachyarrhythmias; ablation targets the critical isthmus (slow conduction zone) to eliminate the circuit.
goals: Understand re-entry as the mechanism for most sustained arrhythmias
Explain AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) as a model

primary_example.explanation:
AVNRT is the most common regular supraventricular tachycardia: (1) Anatomy: AV node has dual pathways—fast pathway (rapid conduction, long refractory period) and slow pathway (slow conduction, short refractory period). (2) Initiation: A premature atrial beat encounters the fast pathway refractory but conducts down the slow pathway (slow conduction). By the time the impulse reaches the distal end, the fast pathway has recovered and conducts retrogradely, re-entering the slow pathway. (3) ECG: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia (150-250 bpm), P waves often buried in QRS or immediately after. (4) Termination: Vagal maneuvers (increase parasympathetic tone) slow AV node conduction, terminating re-entry. (5) Treatment: Acute—vagal maneuvers, adenosine (blocks AV node). Definitive—catheter ablation of slow pathway (cures >95%).

Significance: AVNRT illustrates the re-entry mechanism, the role of dual AV nodal physiology, and the curative potential of catheter ablation targeting the slow pathway.

image.alt: AV node dual pathways (fast and slow). Re-entry circuit: antegrade slow pathway, retrograde fast pathway. ECG: regular narrow-complex tachycardia. Ablation target: slow pathway.
image.caption: AV Nodal Re-entrant Tachycardia (AVNRT): Re-entry using dual AV nodal pathways (slow pathway antegrade, fast pathway retrograde). Catheter ablation of the slow pathway is curative.

image.alt: Re-entry circuit: unidirectional block, slow conduction, recovery of excitability. Anatomic re-entry (scar-related) and functional re-entry (AVNRT).
image.caption: Re-entry Mechanism: Requirements—circuit, unidirectional block, slow conduction, and recovery. Re-entry underlies most sustained tachyarrhythmias.

process_types.type: Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT) for Heart Failure
process_types.energy_profile: In heart failure with left bundle branch block (LBBB), dyssynchronous ventricular contraction reduces efficiency. CRT (biventricular pacing) resynchronizes contraction, improving cardiac output and reducing mortality.
process_types.direction: LBBB causes delayed activation of the left ventricle (lateral wall activated last) → dyssynchronous contraction → reduced ejection fraction, mitral regurgitation, adverse remodeling. CRT: Leads placed in right ventricle and coronary sinus (left ventricular epicardial lead) → simultaneous or near-simultaneous activation of both ventricles → improved synchrony, increased stroke volume, reverse remodeling.
process_types.function: Improve symptoms, reduce hospitalizations, reduce mortality in select heart failure patients (EF ≤35%, QRS ≥150 ms with LBBB).
goals: Understand the pathophysiology of dyssynchrony in heart failure
process_types.energy_source: Heart failure, cardiac electrophysiology, device therapy

primary_example.explanation:
Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) is a device-based treatment for heart failure: (1) Indications: NYHA class II-IV heart failure, LVEF ≤35%, QRS duration ≥150 ms with LBBB (or ≥130 ms with LBBB for some guidelines). (2) Mechanism: CRT restores synchronous contraction by pacing both ventricles (or left ventricle alone with AV optimization). (3) Benefits: Improved LVEF, reduced mitral regurgitation, reverse remodeling (decreased LV dimensions), improved symptoms (NYHA class), reduced hospitalizations, reduced mortality. (4) Device: Biventricular pacemaker (CRT-P) or biventricular defibrillator (CRT-D). (5) Non-responders: Approximately 30% do not respond; causes include suboptimal lead placement, atrial arrhythmias, or non-LBBB morphology.

Significance: CRT demonstrates how understanding the electrical activation sequence (LBBB) and its mechanical consequences (dyssynchrony) can be corrected by electrical therapy, improving heart failure outcomes.

image.alt: ECG: LBBB (wide QRS). CRT: RV lead (apex) and LV lead (coronary sinus) for biventricular pacing. Hemodynamic improvement: increased EF, reverse remodeling.
image.caption: Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT): Biventricular pacing corrects dyssynchronous contraction in LBBB heart failure, improving EF, symptoms, and survival.

image.alt: LBBB activation pattern: delayed LV activation → dyssynchronous contraction → reduced EF. CRT restores synchrony → improved contraction.
image.caption: Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB): Delayed LV activation causes dyssynchrony, reducing ejection fraction. CRT resynchronizes contraction, improving mechanical efficiency.

sections.relationship: The cardiac cycle integrates electrical and mechanical events. The conduction system initiates and propagates the electrical impulse; the ECG records these events; the mechanical phases (contraction, ejection, filling) follow; and heart rate and contractility are modulated by autonomic inputs. Arrhythmias arise from disorders of impulse formation or conduction; understanding these mechanisms guides antiarrhythmic drug therapy, catheter ablation, and device therapy (pacemakers, ICDs, CRT).

summary:
The cardiac cycle is the sequence of electrical and mechanical events in one heartbeat, driven by the heart's intrinsic conduction system and modulated by the autonomic nervous system.

Electrical Conduction: SA node (primary pacemaker, 60-100 bpm) initiates impulses that spread through atria (P wave) to AV node (delay, PR interval), then through His-Purkinje system to ventricles (QRS). Ventricular repolarization produces the T wave. The fibrous cardiac skeleton electrically insulates atria from ventricles except at AV node.

Cardiac Cycle Phases: Atrial systole (atrial kick, 20-30% filling) → isovolumetric contraction (AV valves close, S1) → rapid ejection → reduced ejection → isovolumetric relaxation (semilunar valves close, S2) → rapid filling → diastasis. Pressure gradients determine valve opening and closure.

Excitation-Contraction Coupling: Action potential → L-type Ca²⁺ influx → Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release from SR → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → contraction. Relaxation requires Ca²⁺ reuptake into SR (SERCA2a). Sympathetic stimulation (β1) increases contractility (inotropy) and relaxation (lusitropy).

Frank-Starling Mechanism: Increased end-diastolic volume (preload) increases stroke volume, matching output to venous return. In heart failure, the Frank-Starling curve is flattened; diuretics reduce preload, inotropes increase contractility.

Autonomic Regulation: Sympathetic (β1) increases heart rate (chronotropy), conduction (dromotropy), and contractility (inotropy). Parasympathetic (vagal) decreases heart rate and AV conduction. β-blockers reduce sympathetic overactivity in heart failure.

Arrhythmias: Disorders of impulse formation (automaticity, triggered activity) or conduction (re-entry). Re-entry requires a circuit, unidirectional block, slow conduction, and recovery. AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) is a common supraventricular tachycardia; atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained arrhythmia; ventricular tachycardia may degenerate to ventricular fibrillation.

ECG: Records cardiac electrical activity. ST elevation indicates myocardial injury (STEMI). QRS widening (>120 ms) indicates bundle branch block or ventricular origin. PR prolongation indicates AV conduction delay (first-degree block). Complete heart block (third-degree) shows AV dissociation.

Clinical Applications: Pacemakers treat symptomatic bradyarrhythmias (heart block, sick sinus). Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) prevent sudden death in patients at risk for ventricular arrhythmias. Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) improves outcomes in heart failure with LBBB. Catheter ablation cures many re-entrant arrhythmias (AVNRT, atrial flutter, some VTs).

Understanding the cardiac cycle—from SA node firing to ventricular ejection—is essential for interpreting ECG, managing arrhythmias, optimizing hemodynamics in heart failure, and understanding cardiovascular physiology.
